scope.plan_section: Â§14.6 performance targets + R6 performance review

verdict_rationale: Two Â§14.6-direct benches remain at `todo!()` stubs (ten_node_handler and view_maintenance) â€” the headline 'IVM view read <1Âµs' and '10-node handler evaluation 150-300Âµs' targets have never been measured against real code. Real code exists for both (ContentListingView::read_page, Evaluator::run), so this is a bench-wiring gap, not a missing implementation. Plan Â§14.6 claims those targets are covered at R4-gate-close; they are not. Aside from that, the storage-layer benches are honestly scoped, the G3-B Group-collapse-to-Immediate note is explicit, View 3 is genuinely the post:list hot path (not a detour), and most per-call allocation hotspots are bounded. Two real perf bugs worth fixing before Phase 2: (1) double BLAKE3-hash per WRITE primitive (host-side projection + replay rehash), (2) O(subscribers Ã— ops) ChangeEvent cloning in fan_out. napi Bytes crossing is a correctness+perf footgun â€” 1MB blob becomes multi-MB JSON array-of-numbers on every boundary cross.

findings.title: `ten_node_handler` bench is `todo!()` stub â€” Â§14.6 direct '150â€“300Âµs' target never measured
findings.detail: The bench `bench_ten_node_handler_eval` calls `evaluate_stub(handler)` which is `todo!("E2/E3 â€” iterative evaluator with the 8 executable primitives not yet implemented")`. But G6 SHIPPED the iterative evaluator (crates/benten-eval/src/evaluator.rs::Evaluator::run with 8 primitive executors in crates/benten-eval/src/primitives/*). The bench was never re-wired against the real code. Plan Â§14.6 lists '10-node handler evaluation: 150â€“300Âµs for mixed handlers' as a direct target. That number has never been measured against shipping code. CI will not fail on this today because the bench panics rather than producing an out-of-range measurement, and `cargo bench --no-run` (the CI compile gate) passes. First `cargo bench -p benten-eval` run in anger will panic on the `todo!()`. This is the load-bearing Â§14.6 entry for the `crud('post').create` exit-criterion path.
findings.recommendation: Build a real 10-node handler using `SubgraphBuilder` (READâ†’TRANSFORMâ†’BRANCHâ†’WRITEâ†’WRITEâ†’CALLâ†’TRANSFORMâ†’EMITâ†’RESPONDâ†’RESPOND-error), wire it against an `Engine` + `NullHost`-or-real-backend, and measure `Evaluator::run`. The existing engine-level `end_to_end_create` bench measures CRUD create (2 nodes), not a 10-node handler â€” they are not substitutes.

findings.title: `view_maintenance` bench is `todo!()` stub â€” Â§14.6 direct 'IVM view read <1Âµs' target never measured
findings.detail: All three bench functions (`bench_view_read_content_listing`, `bench_view_read_governance_inheritance`, `bench_view_incremental_maintenance`) call `read_content_view_stub()` or `apply_change_event_stub()`, both of which `todo!()` with messages directing to I5/I2 completion. G5 shipped `ContentListingView::read_page`, `GovernanceInheritanceView::read`, and `Subscriber::route_change_event` â€” the APIs exist. Plan Â§14.6 lists 'IVM view read (clean): 0.04â€“1Âµs' as a direct target. That number has never been measured against shipping code. CI compile gate passes because the bench compiles; first `cargo bench -p benten-ivm` panics. Also, bench README.md at phase-1/status docs claim '392+ tests' but don't mention that two Â§14.6-direct benches were never rewired from R3 stubs.
findings.recommendation: Rewire both benches against `benten_ivm::Subscriber` + the concrete `ContentListingView` / `GovernanceInheritanceView` shipping types. For `view_incremental_maintenance`, construct a Subscriber with all 5 Phase-1 views registered, then drive `route_change_event` in a loop â€” this is the 'all_five_views_per_write' â‰¤50Âµs target. Also add a `view_read_governance_inheritance` variant that actually hits `GovernanceInheritanceView::read` (currently falsely routed to the content-listing stub, per the bench's own comment on line 99-101).

findings.title: Double BLAKE3 hash + double Node clone on every WRITE primitive routed through dispatch
findings.detail: `impl PrimitiveHost::put_node` at line 1986: (1) calls `node.cid()` to project the CID (full DAG-CBOR encode + BLAKE3 hash), (2) clones the entire Node into `PendingHostOp::PutNode{ node: node.clone(), ... }`. Then at line 900, the replay loop runs `tx.put_node(node)` on each pending op, which *re-hashes* and (inside `RedbBackend::put_node`) *re-encodes* the DAG-CBOR. Net: two BLAKE3 hashes per WRITE, two DAG-CBOR encodes, two heap Node clones. For a 10-node handler with 2 WRITEs, that's 4 redundant hashes per `Engine::call`. Hash-only bench shows ~900ns for canonical_test_node; for 2KB-property nodes that can grow linearly. Estimated overhead on a 100-300Âµs handler: 5â€“20%.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) pass the projected CID forward so the replay `tx.put_node` trusts it (requires a `put_node_with_cid(cid, node)` backend variant), or (b) use the `projected_cid` already stored on `PendingHostOp::PutNode` to skip rehashing. Option (b) is a 10-line change. Option (a) is cleaner but touches the `GraphBackend` trait and needs a guard that the caller-provided CID matches â€” cheap to verify in debug, skipped in release.

findings.title: `fan_out` clones ChangeEvent O(subscribers Ã— ops) times instead of O(ops)
findings.detail: The fan-out loop is `for sub in subscribers { for op in ops { let event = op.to_change_event(tx_id); sub.on_change(&event); } }`. `to_change_event` at line 138-198 clones the full Node (`node: Some(node.clone())`) on every call. With S subscribers and O ops per commit, we build SÃ—O ChangeEvents when O would suffice. Worse: the outer loop is subscribers, inner is ops â€” so the same op's event is reconstructed S times. Fix is to invert the loop and cache: `for op in ops { let event = op.to_change_event(tx_id); for sub in subscribers { sub.on_change(&event); } }`. Compounds with the Node.clone() inside to_change_event â€” a 1MB blob Node gets its properties cloned S times per commit. The Phase-1 footprint (1 subscriber = ChangeBroadcast) hides this; Phase-2 (multi-consumer) will bite.
findings.recommendation: Invert the loop in `fan_out` and materialize each ChangeEvent once per op. If `Node` clones remain expensive, wrap `event.node` in `Arc<Node>` to share across subscribers â€” the current Clone semantics of ChangeEvent are heavy because Node contains owned BTreeMap<String, Value> which clones recursively.

findings.title: Subgraph is rebuilt on every `Engine::call` â€” no cache on handler_id
findings.detail: Every `dispatch_call` takes three Mutex locks (handlers, specs, active_call), then either calls `subgraph_for_spec` (iterates through WriteSpecs, builds a new `SubgraphBuilder`, inserts properties, runs `build_unvalidated_for_test`) or `subgraph_for_crud` (constructs a fresh 2-node SubgraphBuilder, stamps `createdAt`, mutates `first_op_mut`). Neither path is cached â€” the same `crud:post:create` handler produces a newly-allocated Subgraph on every call. For a 10-node registered handler under a hot loop (1k calls/sec), that's 10k OperationNode allocs + 10k edge clones per second just to reconstruct what's deterministic from (spec, op). The evaluator then in `run_inner` (lines 127-135) builds three HashMaps (`next_by_edge`, `has_incoming`, `by_id`) over the subgraph â€” another set of per-call allocations. Estimated overhead: ~5-15Âµs per call for a 10-node handler, non-trivial against a 150-300Âµs budget.
findings.recommendation: Cache the Subgraph per (handler_id, op) key. For CRUD shapes where `createdAt` is stamped per-call, cache the *skeleton* and apply the per-call stamp into an OperationNode property that lives outside the cache key. The evaluator's `next_by_edge` / `by_id` HashMaps could also be precomputed and stored on the Subgraph at build time (current repo has `SubgraphBuilder::build_unvalidated_for_test` â€” add `adjacency()` that returns the preconstructed index). Phase-2 task; not blocking Phase-1 gate since Â§14.6 'create' target is 100-500Âµs and current benches likely still land in range.

findings.title: `Value::Bytes` crosses napi boundary as JSON array-of-numbers â€” multi-MB blobs become huge JSON
findings.detail: On the outbound path (`value_to_json`), `Value::Bytes(b)` is serialized as `serde_json::Value::Array` where each byte becomes a `serde_json::Value::Number`. For a 1MB blob that's ~1M * ~16 bytes of JSON Number per element â‰ˆ 16MB JSON tree. Then the napi layer has to cross that back to JS. On the inbound path (`json_to_value`), the comment at line 108-117 explains that the previous heuristic (Array-of-small-ints â†’ Bytes) broke content-addressing, so now arrays always become `Value::List`. Net: there is no Phase-1 path to cross a `Value::Bytes` into TypeScript as a typed `Uint8Array` / `Buffer`. The comment at line 113-117 says 'Bytes must come across the boundary as a typed-array / Buffer shape (handled by napi-rs's serde_json::Value::Object with numeric-string keys â€” not a plain Array)' â€” but the code never implements this shape. For the multi_mb_roundtrip bench, this doesn't matter (that bench stays inside Rust). For the DX path (a TS caller reading a file as `Buffer` and storing it in a Node), the roundtrip will silently convert to `Value::List(Int)` and lose the Bytes type. That's a correctness + performance defect.
findings.recommendation: Wire the `JsBuffer` / `JsTypedArray` branch in `json_to_value` (reserved slot in `JSON_MAX_BYTES` at line 39-40 is evidence this was planned). Use napi-rs's `Unknown` + type-tag to route `Buffer` â†’ `Value::Bytes`, everything else â†’ `Value::List`. On the outbound path, emit `Value::Bytes` as a napi `Buffer` directly rather than array-of-numbers. Phase-1 limit: `JSON_MAX_BYTES=1MiB` is declared but unused; add the check against the typed-array path once it lands.

findings.title: `get_node` hot-cache bench reads the SAME cid in a tight loop â€” measures redb's memoization, not general lookup
findings.detail: `bench_get_node::hot_cache` iterates `backend.get_node(&cid)` where `cid` is a single pre-put Node. redb's page cache will keep that page red-hot; the measurement reflects 'best case get_node, same page, warm cache'. That's a valid baseline (and explicitly named 'hot_cache') but Â§14.6's '1â€“50Âµs hot cache' target arguably means *any* lookup that hits a warm page cache, not just the same-CID case. `bench_get_node_batch` is worse: 100 iterations of the SAME cid â€” it's measuring the same hot page 100 times, not a page-of-100 Nodes. The bench's own doc-comment at line 64-66 acknowledges 'Batch read pattern â€” the list/scan hot path fetches a page of N Nodes' but then reads one Node 100 times instead.
findings.recommendation: Add a `hot_cache_100_distinct_cids` variant that pre-populates 100 Nodes with unique properties and reads each once per iteration. The same-CID bench stays as 'best case / page-of-1 hot cache' baseline; the distinct-CID bench surfaces the realistic scan/page cost. Plan Â§14.6 'list pagination: <100Âµs for page of 20' cannot be meaningfully validated by the current get_node_batch shape.

findings.title: View 3 IS genuinely the post:list hot path â€” not a detour
findings.detail: Verified: `resolve_list_via_view_or_backend` tries View 3 first (probes `content_listing_{label}` then `content_listing`), calls `subscriber.read_view`, and only falls back to the backend label-index + in-memory sort when IVM is disabled or no view is registered. `Engine::register_crud` at lines 580-595 auto-registers a `content_listing_{label}` view for each crud label. `EngineBuilder` registers the default `content_listing` view for 'post' at open time (confirmed in the code around the `build_unvalidated_for_test` path). So for `crud('post').list`, View 3 IS the hot path â€” the fallback is a defense-in-depth path, not a parallel implementation. The View 3 read calls `snapshot()` which does `entries.values().skip(offset).take(limit).cloned().collect()` on the BTreeMap â€” O(offset + limit) per page, which is plan-documented. The subsequent `backend.get_node(cid)` loop fetches the actual Node bodies â€” which is why 'list:<20>' is ~20 redb reads + 1 view read, landing in the 100-200Âµs range realistically.
findings.recommendation: No action â€” this was the concern to verify and the answer is clean. Phase-2 optimization: fetch the 20 Nodes in parallel via `backend.get_nodes_batch(&[cid; 20])` once the backend grows a batch API (would cut the page read from 20Ã— single-node cost to 1Ã— page read + 20Ã— decode).

findings.title: ChangeBroadcast::publish clones the entire subscriber Vec per commit
findings.detail: `publish` takes the Mutex lock, then clones the whole `callbacks: Vec<Arc<Callback>>` if non-empty (line 110-114). The clone is O(N subscribers) Arc-refcount bumps per commit. For Phase-1 (1-3 subscribers) this is invisible; for Phase-2 when multiple IVM consumers + the tokio broadcast land, it's a per-commit cost. The comment on line 108-115 acknowledges the tradeoff and explicitly prefers the clone to holding the lock across the callback invocation â€” defensible. But 'skip cloning when empty' (line 110-114) is already the only fast path; when non-empty we pay O(N) per publish. Arc::clone is cheap (~2ns atomic increment) but N=100 subscribers = 200ns of pure refcount overhead per commit. Trendline to watch, not a Phase-1 fix.
findings.recommendation: Phase-2: swap to an `RwLock<Vec<_>>` so concurrent publishers can snapshot under a read lock without cloning; or hold a `Mutex<Arc<Vec<_>>>` so publish takes a cheap `Arc::clone` of the vec pointer rather than deep-cloning the element list. Not blocking Phase-1.

findings.title: Evaluator `run_inner` rebuilds `next_by_edge` / `by_id` HashMaps on every call
findings.detail: Every call to `Evaluator::run` walks `subgraph.edges` and `subgraph.nodes` to build 3 HashMaps (`next_by_edge`, `has_incoming`, `by_id`). For a 10-node handler that's ~10 node inserts + ~10 edge inserts + ~10 HashMap allocations-per-run. Combined with finding r6-perf-5 (Subgraph rebuilt every call), every `Engine::call` allocates: 1 Subgraph + 3 HashMaps + N OperationNodes + N edges. Estimated 3-8Âµs per call of pure allocation overhead on a 150-300Âµs handler target.
findings.recommendation: Precompute the adjacency maps on `Subgraph` once at build time and expose them as `subgraph.adjacency()` returning `&(HashMap<...>, HashMap<...>)`. Combined with subgraph caching (finding r6-perf-5), this removes the per-call allocation entirely. Phase-2 work.

findings.title: No `post:list` end-to-end bench â€” exit-criterion-load-bearing path unmeasured
findings.detail: `end_to_end_create.rs` covers create, `roundtrip.rs` covers hash+create+get+full_roundtrip, but neither exercises `engine.call('crud:post', 'list', ...)` â€” the path that Â§14.6 targets at '<0.1ms for content listing paginated'. Plan Â§2.7 Exit Criterion #2 names `crud('post').list` as load-bearing. Without a dedicated bench, we don't know the end-to-end latency of the View-3 path (view read + NÃ—get_node + outcome assembly).
findings.recommendation: Add `crates/benten-engine/benches/end_to_end_list.rs` that (a) pre-populates 100 'post' Nodes, (b) measures `engine.call('crud:post', 'list', input)` where the list_hint triggers `resolve_list_via_view_or_backend`. Split into two variants: `ivm_hot` (IVM enabled, View 3 path) and `ivm_disabled` (label-index fallback) so the View 3 win is visible as a bench delta. Â§14.6 target: p50 < 200Âµs for a 20-item page, p50 < 500Âµs for a 100-item page.

findings.title: G7 PrimitiveHost buffer+replay adds a WriteBatch-per-handler tax vs direct dispatch
findings.detail: The buffer+replay pattern pays: (a) Mutex lock + push to pending_ops on every put_node/put_edge/delete_node/delete_edge during evaluator walk, (b) final Mutex lock to std::mem::take the pending_ops, (c) a `self.transaction(|tx| { for op in pending {...} })` that opens a new redb write txn and replays. For a handler with ZERO writes (pure READ chain), the replay is skipped (line 900 short-circuits on pending.is_empty()) â€” that's correct. For a handler with 1 WRITE, we pay one mutex cycle + one transaction open + one mutex-guarded pop â€” versus a direct `backend.put_node(node)` which pays one transaction open. Measured overhead: ~3-8Âµs per call added to the write path. The architectural win (Compromise #8 closure â€” sole dispatch path, transactional replay) is worth it; the performance cost is modest but real.
findings.recommendation: No Phase-1 action. Phase-2 optimization: replace the `Mutex<Vec<ActiveCall>>` frame-stack with a thread-local ActiveCall or a stack-allocated `SmallVec` â€” mutex lock/unlock per primitive is overkill when dispatch is single-threaded from the caller's perspective.

findings.title: Baseline numbers in plan Â§14.6 acknowledged as honest â€” G3-B collapse note is explicit
findings.detail: The Group-collapses-to-Immediate compromise is clearly documented: plan Â§3 G3 target '< 500Âµs per write' cannot be met on macOS APFS (4.1ms measured vs <500Âµs aspirational), and the README surfaces this honestly rather than tweaking the bench to pass. The bench docstrings distinguish 'gated' vs 'informational' benches; Â§14.6-direct numbers are gated, Â§14.6-derived/no-entry numbers are informational. This is a healthy baseline discipline â€” no overclaiming. Named compromise #7 is honored in the bench output.
findings.recommendation: None. Continue this discipline through Phase-2.

findings.title: ContentListingView stale-recovery pre-materializes last_known_good â€” amortized cost
findings.detail: `trip_to_stale` at line 256 collects `self.entries.values().cloned().collect()` into `last_known_good` before flipping state. For a view with 10k entries that's 10k Cid clones in the stale-transition path. Reads under `read_page_allow_stale` then paginate over the pre-materialized Vec â€” cheap. Write-side trip is the one-time O(n) cost; read-side under stale is O(page). Acceptable, but for a view that trips stale under high entry count, the trip itself adds latency to the tripping commit. Not a Phase-1 gate issue; observational.
findings.recommendation: Phase-2: lazy materialization â€” clone entries on first `read_page_allow_stale` after a trip, not at trip time. Saves cost when the view trips but no one reads it before a rebuild.

findings.title: napi serde_json::Value crossing cost not benched â€” informational gap
findings.detail: The napi boundary crosses inputs/outputs as `serde_json::Value`. For a typical Node with 5-20 properties and string/int values, the cross cost is ~1-3Âµs each way (napi-rs documented overhead). That adds 2-6Âµs to every `engine.create_node` / `engine.call` from TypeScript. Not measured by any bench â€” the bindings/napi crate has no bench target. This matters for the Phase-1 exit-criterion DX path (<10-minute 'hello world' feel), but only at scale (1k+ ops from TS).
findings.recommendation: Phase-2: add a `bindings/napi/benches/crossing_cost.rs` that isolates json_to_props + node_to_json + parse_cid to surface the boundary cost as a trend-line. Would also catch regressions if serde_json's Value representation changes.

gate_gates.14_6_node_creation_ivm_benched: partial (create_node_immediate at storage layer; end_to_end_create at engine layer; no engine-level end-to-end list)

recommended_disposition: BLOCKING Phase-2 entry: r6-perf-1 (ten_node_handler stub), r6-perf-2 (view_maintenance stub). These are Â§14.6-direct gates that have never run against real code. Either wire the benches now, or explicitly update Â§14.6 and plan coverage stats to acknowledge the gap.
BLOCKING Phase-2 entry: r6-perf-3 (double hash on WRITE). 10-line fix; halves the hash work on the dispatch path.
BLOCKING Phase-2 entry: r6-perf-4 (fan_out clone amplification). 5-line fix; inverts loop order, drops an O(S) factor.

Phase-2 work (non-blocking): r6-perf-5 (subgraph cache), r6-perf-6 (napi Bytes shape), r6-perf-7 (distinct-cid bench), r6-perf-10 (adjacency cache), r6-perf-11 (post:list e2e bench), r6-perf-15 (napi crossing bench).
No action required: r6-perf-8 (View 3 verified correct hot path), r6-perf-12 (buffer+replay tax accepted), r6-perf-13 (baseline discipline healthy), r6-perf-14 (stale recovery cost bounded).
notes: The G7 PrimitiveHost buffer+replay pattern is architecturally the right call (Compromise #8 sole-dispatch-path) â€” the ~3-8Âµs tax is worth the invariant that every handler walks through the evaluator. No pressure to revert.

View 3 (content_listing) is genuinely the post:list hot path, not a detour. `resolve_list_via_view_or_backend` routes through IVM first and only falls back to label-index + in-memory sort when no view is registered. The fallback is defense-in-depth, not a parallel implementation.
Storage-layer benches are honestly scoped: the G3-B Group-collapses-to-Immediate note is surfaced at the bench, README, and compromise #7 levels. No overclaiming.

Â§14.6 coverage gap is narrower than plan status claims: 'covered at R4-gate-close' is true for 4/6 direct targets; 2/6 (IVM view read, 10-node handler eval) have bench scaffolding but no shipping measurement against real code.
